notable.notable_blurb: Co-founded Debevoise & Plimpton; defended Alger Hiss

narrative_blocks.header: Named for his great-grandfather the cotton-gin inventor
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Manhattan in 1899 to Anne Farnam Whitney and Thomas McElrath Debevoise, who served as John D. Rockefeller Jr.'s longtime attorney. His mother named him for her great-grandfather, the cotton-gin inventor whose Whitney Armory had founded Whitneyville, Connecticut a century earlier.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Debevoise & Plimpton with $2,366
narrative_blocks.body: In 1931, after six years at Davis Polk & Wardwell, he and fellow associate William Stevenson formed a partnership under the name Debevoise & Stevenson, with $2,366 in their account. Francis Plimpton joined them shortly after, and the firm — eventually Debevoise & Plimpton — became one of New York's most prominent international law practices.

narrative_blocks.header: Defended Alger Hiss in his perjury trials
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Alger Hiss at his 1949-1950 perjury trials stemming from the Hiss-Chambers House Un-American Activities Committee testimony, one of the highest-profile cases of the early Cold War. He also served as legal adviser to Ford Motor Company as it became a publicly traded corporation in 1956.

narrative_blocks.header: Deputy High Commissioner for occupied Germany
narrative_blocks.body: From 1951 to 1953 he served as Deputy High Commissioner for Germany and general counsel to the Allied Commission that administered Germany's affairs in the years after World War II. He returned to Debevoise & Plimpton in 1953 and remained active in international human rights work for the rest of his career.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded the International Commission of Jurists
narrative_blocks.body: After his return from Germany he co-founded the International Commission of Jurists, one of the first global human rights organizations, and chaired Governor Nelson Rockefeller's 1967-1968 Committee to Review New York Laws and Procedures on Human Rights. He retired at age 87 and died in Manhattan in 1990 at age ninety.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-five-year champion of New York's Lighthouse
narrative_blocks.body: He spent over twenty-five years of his life on the New York Association for the Blind — the Lighthouse — including five years as its president from 1946 to 1951. He believed the institution's mission of teaching the blind self-reliance applied equally to delinquent youth, a conviction that led to his 1959 appointment heading New York State's task force on youth crime.

cross_connections.display_label: Father served as JDR Jr.'s longtime attorney

institutions.institution_blurb: Trustee of the American Geographical Society.

career.notes: Firm headed by John W. Davis, the 1924 Democratic presidential candidate.

Co-founded with William Stevenson in 1931 with $2,366 in their account. Joined by Francis Plimpton, then Robert G. Page in 1936. Early success as counsel to the Ivar Kreuger International Match Company bankruptcy 1932.
World War II civilian post.
General counsel to the Allied Commission administering occupied West Germany after WWII.
One of the first global human rights organizations.

career.organization: Gov. Nelson Rockefeller's Committee to Review NY Laws and Procedures on Human Rights
career.notes: Final report delivered March 1968.

career.role: Teacher of medieval and ancient history
career.notes: Taught between Yale graduation 1921 and Harvard Law enrollment 1923. Returned to his secondary alma mater. Lifelong interest in history persisted into international relations specialty.
His father Thomas McElrath Debevoise was general counsel to the Institute.

career.organization: New York Association for the Blind (the Lighthouse)
career.notes: Served as president 1946-1951; was active for 25+ years by 1959. Believed the Lighthouse's mission of building self-reliance applied equally to youth crime prevention.

career.role: Head of Task Force on Youth Crime
career.notes: Per 1959 NYT profile — appointment built on his Lighthouse experience with self-reliance training.

parents.father_research_notes: Yale Professor of Archaeology and Ancient Languages (specific name not yet researched). Per 1959 NYT profile of Eli Whitney Debevoise.

education.notes: Left Conn. College in 1923 to transfer to Radcliffe.

death.cause: lung cancer (complications from surgery, Dartmouth-Hitchcock Medical Center)

notable.notable_blurb: Vermont Attorney General; Vermont Law School Dean

career.notes: Assisted James B. Donovan in the defense of Soviet spy Rudolf Abel — the case Donovan later used as leverage in the 1962 prisoner swap returning U-2 pilot Francis Gary Powers.
Appointed January 1959; served until promoted to Attorney General February 1960.
Appointed Feb 2, 1960 by Gov. Robert Stafford to fill vacancy of Frederick M. Reed; won full term Nov 1960 as Republican; resigned Jan 1, 1962. Succeeded by Charles J. Adams.

career.location: New York City and Washington, D.C.
career.notes: Distinct from his father's Debevoise & Plimpton.

career.role: Trustee Board President / Dean / Dean Emeritus
career.notes: Trustee board president 1973-1974; Dean 1974-1982 (founding-era leadership of the school established in 1972); Dean Emeritus 1982-1995; trustee emeritus 1983-1995. Honorary Doctor of Laws 1984. Debevoise Hall on the VLS campus (renovated 2005) was named for him and his wife Ann.
Foundation established by Laurance Rockefeller and Mary French Rockefeller to promote conservation and sustainable land use.

narrative_blocks.header: Helped defend Soviet spy Rudolf Abel
narrative_blocks.body: As a young Assistant U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York 1954-1956, he assisted James B. Donovan in the court-appointed defense of accused Soviet spy Col. Rudolf Abel. Donovan later used the case as leverage in the 1962 prisoner swap that returned downed U-2 pilot Francis Gary Powers in exchange for Abel's release.

narrative_blocks.header: Appointed Vermont Attorney General at thirty
narrative_blocks.body: After moving to Woodstock in 1956 and serving as Deputy Attorney General from 1959, he was appointed Vermont Attorney General in February 1960 by Governor Robert Stafford to fill a vacancy. He won a full term as a Republican that November and served until resigning in January 1962.

narrative_blocks.header: Founding-era Dean of Vermont Law School
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Dean of the Vermont Law School from 1974 to 1982, leading the institution through its formative first decade after its 1972 founding. Vermont Law School awarded him an honorary Doctor of Laws in 1984; Debevoise Hall on the South Royalton campus, renovated in 2005, was named for him and his wife Ann.

narrative_blocks.header: Led the Rockefellers' Woodstock Foundation thirteen years
narrative_blocks.body: From 1982 until his death in 1995 he served as president of the Woodstock Foundation, established by Laurance Rockefeller and Mary French Rockefeller to promote conservation and sustainable land use. He had moved to Woodstock in 1956 and made it his home for the rest of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Lung cancer at sixty-five at Dartmouth-Hitchcock
narrative_blocks.body: He died February 1, 1995 at the Dartmouth-Hitchcock Medical Center in Lebanon, New Hampshire of complications from surgery for lung cancer. He was survived by his wife Ann, his sister Elizabeth Debevoise Healy of Manhattan, four children, and eight grandchildren.

notable.notable_blurb: Arnold & Porter partner; US World Bank director

bio.bio_blurb: World Bank director; Arnold & Porter partner

narrative_blocks.header: America’s man on the World Bank board
narrative_blocks.body: As U.S. Executive Director of the World Bank Group from 2007 to 2009, he led the bank’s first capital increase in twenty-two years and its share reallocation. Brazil had already given him the Order of Rio Branco; asked his ideal second career, he answered “history professor.”

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the cotton gin’s inventor
narrative_blocks.body: Eli Whitney was his great-great-great-great-grandfather, and the name came down the line intact. Asked his ideal second career, the Arnold & Porter partner answered: “History professor.”

narrative_blocks.header: Brazil gave him the Rio Branco
narrative_blocks.body: Brazil awarded him the Order of Rio Branco in 1997; a decade later, as America’s executive director at the World Bank, he led the institution’s first capital increase in twenty-two years.

career.notes: Associate at his father-in-law Eli Whitney Debevoise's firm per 1959 NYT profile.

notable.notable_blurb: John D. Rockefeller Jr.'s longtime attorney

cross_connections.display_label: Served as JDR Jr.'s longtime personal attorney
both were lawyers to the Rockefeller family

narrative_blocks.header: John D. Rockefeller Jr.'s longtime attorney
narrative_blocks.body: He served as the personal attorney to John D. Rockefeller Jr. for many years, advising on the philanthropist's extensive business and charitable affairs. The relationship gave the Debevoise family a direct professional tie to one of the era's most consequential American fortunes.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Whitney cotton-gin family
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1890s he married Anne Farnam Whitney, the eldest daughter of Eli Whitney IV and great-granddaughter of the cotton-gin inventor. Their only son, Eli Whitney Debevoise, was named for the inventor and would later co-found the law firm Debevoise & Plimpton.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived his wife by less than two years
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Anne died in February 1957. He died December 20, 1958 in Summit, New Jersey at age eighty-four. Both are buried at Fair Mount Cemetery in Chatham, New Jersey, where their son and his second wife would later be interred as well.

narrative_blocks.header: General counsel to the Rockefeller philanthropic empire
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his role as JDR Jr.'s personal counsel he served as general counsel to the Rockefeller Institute for Medical Research, the Rockefeller Foundation, and the General Education Board — the three pillars of John D. Rockefeller Sr.'s and Jr.'s philanthropic infrastructure. He chaired the distribution committee of the New York Community Trust from its 1923 founding and held his office at 30 Rockefeller Plaza in Manhattan.

career.notes: Practiced under his own name as the named final partner before becoming JDR Jr's personal counsel.

career.location: New York City (30 Rockefeller Plaza office)
career.notes: Principal lawyer and spokesman for JDR Jr. on a wide range of business, civic, and personal matters.
Founded by JDR Sr. 1901 (now Rockefeller University).
Founded by JDR Sr. and JDR Jr. 1913.
Rockefeller education philanthropy founded 1903.
NYCT was founded in 1923 to administer charitable funds.
Paint manufacturing concern; family-named company.
Per 1958 obit, served on multiple bank boards.

orbit_connections.name: Eli Whitney Debevoise
orbit_connections.relationship: Great-great-grandson of Eli Whitney and Henrietta Frances Edwards (HD2046), through Eli Whitney IV (HD2089) → Anne Farnam Whitney → Eli Whitney Debevoise
orbit_connections.notes: Eli Whitney Debevoise (Dec 14, 1899 – Jun 30, 1990). Yale 1921, Harvard Law 1925. Co-founded law firm Debevoise & Plimpton (1931, with $2,366 in their account). Represented Alger Hiss at his perjury trials. WWII: Chairman of the Alien Enemy Hearing Board. Founder of the International Commission of Jurists. Legal counsel to Ford Motor Company as it became a public corporation. His grandson Eli Whitney Debevoise II (b. 1953, Yale 1974, Harvard Law 1977) was US Executive Director of the World Bank 2007-2009. (Wikipedia; FindAGrave)

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, the railroad and canal builder

institutions.institution_blurb: Adjacent museum preserving the Whitney Armory site established 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1869 like his cotton-gin grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1869, his grandfather Eli Whitney had graduated Yale Phi Beta Kappa in 1792. He continued the family tradition of New Haven engineering and civic leadership.

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded his father as Water Company president
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited his father's role at the New Haven Water Company and ran it as president for nearly four decades. The position made him a central figure in turn-of-the-century New Haven civic life.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven daughters closed the male Whitney line
narrative_blocks.body: He and Sarah Sheffield Farnam had seven daughters but no surviving sons, ending the male line of Whitney inventors and arms makers. Through his daughter Anne the Whitney name passed into the Debevoise family of New York lawyers.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST014.name: American Geographical Society
INST014.notes: Founded 1851 by 31 New Yorkers, originally to organize a search for the lost Franklin Polar Expedition. Among the founders: Henry Evelyn Pierrepont (HD1533, gen 6) and Rev. Joshua Leavitt (HD0867, gen 6) — two Hooker descendants through entirely separate branches, almost certainly unaware of their shared ancestry. Pierrepont served on its Council 1852-1862. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Geographical_Society

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.

INST018.name: Amherst College
INST018.notes: Founded 1821. One of the oldest liberal arts colleges in the United States. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as 6th President 1890-1899. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amherst_College David Peck Todd (HD1758) was astronomy professor and observatory director 1881-1917. Millicent Todd Bingham (HD1759) received honorary LL.D. 1957.
